summary:
Franchthi Cave is an archaeological site in the Argolid region of southern Greece with a continuous occupation sequence spanning from approximately 37,000 BCE to around 3000 BCE, making it one of the most comprehensively documented prehistoric sites in the Aegean. For the period around 10,000–8,000 BCE, Franchthi Cave was occupied by hunter-gatherer-fishers who exploited the coastal and upland resources of the northeastern Peloponnese.

Significantly, the faunal record at Franchthi shows the presence of deep-sea tuna bones from as early as 10,000 BCE, implying that inhabitants undertook open-water fishing voyages — evidence for early seafaring capability in the Aegean. Obsidian from the island of Melos, located some 150 kilometers offshore, appears in the assemblages from around 13,000 BCE onward, confirming that these communities were conducting maritime voyages by this period. Later layers document the introduction of domesticated plants and animals around 7000 BCE, associated with the arrival of farming communities likely originating in Anatolia, providing one of the clearest stratigraphic records of the transition to agriculture in Greece.
